name: CodeLens
description: The Neural Bridge Between Developer & Codebase. A high-fidelity architectural autopilot synthesizing massive repositories into an interactive neural index.

sections.title: Neural RAG Pipeline
sections.content: Engineered a sophisticated Retrieval-Augmented Generation pipeline grounded in source code truth. It transcends simple keyword matching by understanding developer intent through high-dimensional vector synthesis and semantic contextualization.

sections.title: Architectural Discovery
sections.content: A glassmorphic, high-focus interface designed to map out structural integrity and logic flow. From onboarding new developers to auditing complex legacy systems, CodeLens provides the strategic depth required for modern engineering.

sections.title: Incremental SHA-256 Indexing
sections.content: Implemented hash-based delta analysis for codebase ingestion. By tracking file changes with SHA-256, only modified fragments are re-embedded, drastically reducing latency and operational costs for private repository analysis.

learnings: Sub-millisecond vector retrieval is the primary bottleneck for building truly conversational architectural interfaces.
AST-aware chunking outperforms standard character-based splitting by preserving the semantic integrity of classes and functions.
Deploying Next.js 16/React 19 applications requires a 'Server-First' mindset for optimal heavy-lifting AI tasks.

name: FerrumDB
description: High-performance, embedded document database engine written from scratch in Rust. Features O(1) performance, AES-256 encryption, and atomic transactions.

sections.title: Bitcask-Inspired AOF
sections.content: Engineered a custom binary log format using an append-only file (AOF) architecture. This design optimizes for high-throughput sequential I/O, ensuring deterministic recovery and crash-resilience through deterministic log replay.

sections.title: Pluggable I/O Abstraction
sections.content: Implemented a sophisticated AsyncFileSystem trait that abstracts disk operations. This allowed for a decorator-based encryption layer, seamlessly wrapping standard I/O with AES-256-GCM authentication tags for each data block.

sections.title: Ferrum Studio
sections.content: Developed a built-in observability dashboard using Axum and lock-free atomic metrics. It provides real-time telemetry on operations per second, lookup counts, and database health metrics at sub-microsecond precision.

learnings: Rust's ownership model is uniquely suited for building lock-free storage engines with high concurrency.
The decorator pattern is powerful for layering cross-cutting concerns like encryption and logging without polluting core logic.
Embedded databases must prioritize memory efficiency; O(1) reads come at the cost of ram-resident indexes.

name: ELYX
description: Local-First Autonomous AI Employee. A multi-platform orchestration engine that monitors email, social media, and ERPs to execute business logic via HITL workflows.

sections.title: Perception Layer
sections.content: Engineered a Python-based watching system using Playwright and standard APIs to monitor disjointed communication channels. The layer synthesizes raw platform events into structured Markdown tasks for the reasoning engine.

sections.title: Obsidian-as-Memory
sections.content: Pioneered a 'Local-First' memory architecture using an Obsidian vault. By treating Markdown files as state-machines, ELYX provides deep transparency—every plan, log, and approval cycle is an immutable technical artifact.

sections.title: HITL Approval Cycles
sections.content: Implemented a rigid Human-in-the-Loop (HITL) protocol for sensitive operations. The orchestrator generates execution plans in a 'Pending' state, requiring the user to physically move files in the vault to authorize high-risk actions.

learnings: Autonomous agents are only as reliable as their 'perception' fidelity; handling platform-specific edge cases is the hardest part.
Markdown is a surprisingly robust state management tool for local-first AI systems due to its human-readable nature.
A truly swappable 'Brain Factory' requires strict output normalization to handle different LLM reasoning styles.
